questions.question: Define the 0/1 Knapsack Problem. How does it differ from Fractional Knapsack?
keyPoints: 0/1 Knapsack: each item can be taken at most once (binary choice)
Fractional Knapsack: items can be taken in fractions
0/1 requires DP, Fractional can be solved by Greedy
Example: with weight limit and items having weight-value pairs

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for 0/1 Knapsack and explain its components.
keyPoints: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i])
When weight[i] ≤ w, we have two choices
First term: not including ith item
Second term: including ith item
If weight[i] > w, dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]

questions.question: Explain the time and space complexity of the standard DP approach for Knapsack.
keyPoints: Time complexity: O(n × W) where n=items, W=capacity
Space complexity: O(n × W) for 2D table
Explanation: We fill n×W subproblems, each in O(1) time
Can be optimized to O(W) space using 1D array

questions.question: Why doesn't the Greedy approach work for 0/1 Knapsack? Provide an example.
keyPoints: Greedy chooses based on highest value-to-weight ratio
It doesn't consider the constraint that items can't be split
Greedy picks first two (weight30, value160) vs optimal (all three weight60, value280)
Greedy fails because it doesn't explore all combinations

questions.question: Explain how you would reconstruct the selected items after computing the DP table.
keyPoints: Start from dp[n][W] - bottom right corner
If dp[n][w] ≠ dp[n-1][w], item n was selected
Decrease w by weight[n] and move to dp[n-1][w]
If equal, item n was not selected, move to dp[n-1][w]
Continue until i=0

questions.question: What is memoization? How is it applied to the Knapsack problem?
keyPoints: Memoization: top-down approach storing subproblem results
Avoids recomputation of already solved subproblems
For Knapsack: create memo table initialized with -1
Before computing dp[i][w], check if already computed
Converts exponential O(2^n) to polynomial O(n×W)

questions.question: Differentiate between 0/1 Knapsack and Unbounded Knapsack.
keyPoints: 0/1: each item can be taken at most once
Unbounded: each item can be taken unlimited times
Recurrence differs: 0/1 uses dp[i-1], Unbounded uses dp[i]
0/1 iterates backward, Unbounded iterates forward in space-optimized version

questions.question: Solve the following 0/1 Knapsack problem using dynamic programming: Capacity W=8, Items: (weight, value) = {(2,10), (3,15), (4,40), (5,60)}. Show the DP table and identify the selected items.
keyPoints: Create DP table of size 5×9 (0 to 4 items, 0 to 8 capacity)
Fill table row by row using recurrence

dp[4][8] gives maximum profit = 65 (items with weight 2,3,4 exceeds, so weight 5 and 3 gives 75 exceeds) actually recalculate: best is weight 4(40)+weight 3(15)+weight 2(10)=65 with weight 9 exceeds. Weight 5(60)+weight 2(10)=70 exceeds. Weight 4(40)+weight 3(15)=55 fits. Weight 5(60)+weight 2(10) =70 but weight 7. Wait 60+10=70 weight 7, that's 70! No, weight 5+2=7 fits in 8, value 70. That's the maximum. Selected: items with weight 5 and 2.

questions.question: Explain the space optimization technique for 0/1 Knapsack. Convert the 2D DP solution to a 1D array solution with proper reasoning.
keyPoints: 2D table uses dp[i][w] for first i items
1D array uses dp[w] for current items considered
Key: iterate capacity from W down to weight[i]
Reason: backward iteration ensures each item used only once
Forward iteration would allow same item multiple times
Code: for i in items: for w from W down to weight[i]: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight[i]] + value[i])
Trade-off: cannot reconstruct selected items easily without additional tracking

questions.question: Write an algorithm for 0/1 Knapsack using both memoization (top-down) and tabulation (bottom-up) approaches. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Memoization: recursive with cache, O(n×W) time, O(n×W) space for table + O(n×W) for recursion stack
Tabulation: iterative filling of table, O(n×W) time, O(n×W) space
Memoization only computes needed subproblems, Tabulation computes all
Memoization has overhead of function calls, Tabulation is faster
Memoization easier to implement recursively, Tabulation avoids stack overflow
Both achieve same time complexity, different space usage patterns

questions.question: Prove that 0/1 Knapsack exhibits optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems properties.
keyPoints: Optimal Substructure: If optimal solution includes item i, then the remaining solution must be optimal for capacity W-weight[i] with first i-1 items
Proof by contradiction: if remaining wasn't optimal, we could substitute better solution to get higher value
Overlapping Subproblems: Different paths can lead to same state (i,w)
Example: choosing items {1,3} vs {2,3} both may result in same remaining capacity and items to consider
DP table stores results of these overlapping states, avoiding recomputation
Without these properties, DP would not be applicable
